title: RMA: an Agentic System for Research-Level Mathematical Problems
optimized_headline: Unlocking RMA: A New Approach to Complex Research-Level Math Challenges

raw_body:
arXiv:2605.22875v1 Announce Type: new 
Abstract: We present $\textbf{Research Math Agents (RMA)}$, an agentic framework for automated reasoning on research-level mathematical problems. Unlike prior studies centered on competition mathematics or formal theorem proving, RMA targets research-level mathematical problems that require long-horizon reasoning, literature grounding, and iterative proof refinement. RMA decomposes research-level proof solving into specialized modules for problem analysis, literature search and understanding, fair comparison, knowledge-bank construction, and proof verification, all coordinated by initializer, proposer, and verifier agents through a shared structured memory. Within this unified framework, these agents operate in a multi-role, multi-round workflow, collaboratively generating, refining, and verifying candidate proofs through iterative feedback. We evaluate RMA on the First Proof benchmark, which consists of ten research-level problems contributed by expert mathematicians across diverse domains. Through comprehensive expert evaluation, RMA outperforms strong baselines on the First Proof benchmark, including GPT-5.2R and Aletheia, solving eight out of ten research problems and producing more logically sound and readable proofs. Our comprehensive ablation studies further show that performance gains arise from the interaction of structured reasoning modules, iterative refinement, and verifier-based feedback, rather than any single component. Our solutions and implementations will be made publicly available upon acceptance.
speedrun: The Research Math Agents (RMA) framework introduces a new approach to solving complex mathematical problems that traditional methods struggle with. RMA stands out by utilizing a multi-agent system that breaks down proof-solving into specialized tasks, coordinating efforts to enhance reasoning. In tests against established models like GPT-5.2R, RMA successfully solved 8 out of 10 research problems, demonstrating superior logical soundness and readability. This advancement could significantly impact how mathematical research is conducted and validated.
why_it_matters: Researchers could benefit immediately from RMA's ability to tackle complex problems, streamlining the proof process. This could lead to faster advancements in mathematical knowledge.
The success of RMA indicates a shift toward collaborative AI systems in research, suggesting that future tools may increasingly rely on multi-agent frameworks to enhance problem-solving capabilities.

lenses.eli12: Research Math Agents (RMA) is like a team of specialists working together to solve tough math problems. Each agent has a specific job, like analyzing the problem or checking the proof. By collaborating, they create better solutions than if they worked alone. This could help students and researchers tackle complex math more effectively.
lenses.pm: For product managers and founders, RMA highlights a user need for tools that simplify complex problem-solving in research. By improving efficiency in generating and verifying proofs, RMA could reduce costs associated with research time. This could inspire new features in educational or research-focused products.
lenses.engineer: RMA employs a multi-agent system that decomposes proof-solving into various specialized modules, enhancing its ability to tackle complex mathematical challenges. In evaluations against the First Proof benchmark, RMA outperformed models like GPT-5.2R, solving 8 out of 10 problems with higher logical soundness. The performance gains stem from the interaction of its structured reasoning modules and iterative feedback mechanisms.
